text: The gastrectomy or total gastrectomy is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of gastric cancer when the tumor is located in the top half or the middle of the stomach. When the tumor is in this location, effective treatment requires that we remove the entire stomach as well as any surrounding lymph nodes.
The surgeon prepares to reconnect the small intestine to the esophagus, aiming to restore gastrointestinal continuity following the removal of the stomach.
The surgeon places a feeding tube into the patient's abdomen, ensuring proper positioning to temporarily assist with nutrition following the gastrectomy procedure.

At TriHealth, we perform this surgery minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is of a real total gastrectomy procedure performed at TriHealth. The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical total gastrectomy.
If you are scheduled to have this procedure your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.
The surgeon uses instruments to lift the stomach, exposing the omentum, which is being divided during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon lifts the omentum, a layer of fat hanging off the stomach and connecting to the transverse colon, and divides it using surgical instruments.
The vessel sealer, visible on the right side of the screen, cauterizes and divides tissue during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to divide the omentum using a hook cautery, carefully separating the tissue planes visible around the stomach.
The surgeon lifts the omentum while pushing down the mesentery of the transverse colon, causing it to fall away from the omentum.
The surgeon divides the omentum using a hook cautery, ensuring there are no major blood vessels present. The dissection progresses from the upper part of the stomach towards the distal end.
The surgeon lifts the stomach to access and divide the connective tissue between the stomach and the transverse colon, using surgical instruments to separate these structures.
The surgeon rolls the stomach to access its underside, lifting it up to reveal the gastropoploic artery and vein on the left side, which are major blood vessels supplying the stomach.
The surgeon leaves the blood vessel intact while dissecting down to the base. An enlarged lymph node is visible along the blood vessel, which will be removed with the specimen.
The surgeon lifts the tissue to expose the base of the blood vessels, which are seen diving below the pancreas, ensuring they are included in the resection.

The surgeon manipulates the stomach at the lesser curvature, lifting the stomach tissue while pushing down the pancreas and the hepatic artery, which runs along the top of the pancreas. The camera backs up to provide a clear view of the pancreas.
The surgeon lifts the stomach to reveal a firm area identified as the tumor, which is difficult to grasp. The stomach is then maneuvered in the opposite direction, moving towards the esophagus.
The surgeon lifts the stomach and divides the attachments between the stomach and the underlying pancreas, pushing the pancreas down to separate the tissue connections between these structures.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery, which runs along the top part of the pancreas, and pushes it down. The tissue above the artery is targeted for removal, including important lymph nodes relevant to cancer surgery.
The surgeon identifies the tortuous splenic artery, visible twisting and turning on the right side of the screen. The surrounding tissue is carefully lifted off the artery to expose it further.

The camera backs up to reveal a clear view of the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon continues to divide the attachments between the posterior of the stomach and the pancreas. Once these attachments are divided, the stomach is dropped, and the focus shifts to the lesser curve, visible as the top border of the stomach.

The surgeon navigates above the tumor, identifying the thin veil of tissue known as the lesser omentum, which connects the stomach to the liver. The maroonish brown liver is visible at the top left of the screen.

The surgeon works along the lesser curve of the stomach, dividing tissue to expose the right gastric artery. Using a vessel sealer, the artery is cauterized and cut, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to cut the blood vessels located on the opposite side of the stomach, ensuring cauterization and division of the tissue.
The surgeon uses a hook to carefully create a window around the gastropoploic artery and vein, allowing for complete circumferential access to these vessels.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the vein near the lesser curve of the stomach, ensuring the lymph node seen earlier is included in the specimen.
The surgeon lifts the end of the stomach using umbilical tape to position it for stapling across the intestine, preparing to secure the tissue with a surgical stapler.
The surgeon divides the duodenum, the first portion of the small intestine, just beyond the stomach using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon has completely separated the end of the stomach and is now working back towards the esophagus. The transverse colon is visible on the right side of the screen. The surgeon carefully divides tissue near the transverse colon, ensuring that the surrounding fat is included with the stomach in the specimen.
The surgeon navigates near the spleen, visible at the top right of the screen, closely positioned to the stomach. The left cruce of the diaphragm is also visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the stomach off the diaphragm muscle, visible in the bottom left of the screen, revealing the esophagus and surrounding structures.
The surgeon rolls the stomach off the diaphragm muscle, then flips the stomach to access and manipulate the opposite side.

The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the left gastric artery and left coronary vein, surrounded by numerous lymph nodes. The tissue is divided to ensure the lymph nodes are included with the surgical specimen.
The surgeon lifts the lymph nodes adjacent to the blood vessels, focusing on a pulsing vessel on the left that supplies the liver.
The surgeon carefully peels lymph nodes away from the coronary vein and left gastric artery, ensuring these nodes are included in the surgical specimen while leaving the blood vessels intact.

The surgeon identifies the coronary vein, visible as a purple blood vessel running vertically. The vessel is encircled and prepared for division using a vessel sealer, which will seal and cut the vein.
The surgeon exposes the artery located behind the stomach, carefully dissecting around it. Additional tissue is removed to fully encircle the artery, which is then divided after being cauterized.

After dividing the blood vessels, the surgeon lifts the remaining lymph nodes up and out with the specimen. Below the surgical field, the white tissue of the aorta is visible, and the surgeon carefully lifts surrounding tissue off of it.
The surgeon approaches the top part of the stomach near the esophagus, identifying and preparing to remove a lymph node located along the hepatic artery, known as the hepatic artery lymph node.

The surgeon approaches the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, where the esophagus meets the stomach, and prepares to divide the tissue at this site. A small lymph node near the junction is identified for inclusion in the surgical specimen.
The surgeon pushes down on the diaphragm muscle while lifting the stomach and esophagus up and to the right, separating the tissue attached to the esophagus at the gastroesophageal (GE) junction.

The surgeon identifies the diaphragm muscle and proceeds to lift the esophagus, similar to the previous maneuver with the lower part of the stomach, preparing to separate the remaining tissue around the esophagus.

The surgeon begins dividing the tissue just below the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, leaving a small portion of the stomach attached to prevent retraction into the chest, ensuring better exposure for subsequent steps.

The surgeon places the completely divided specimen, including the stomach and lymph nodes, into a pouch. The hepatic artery lymph node is placed into the bag first, followed by the entire specimen. The bag is then closed and prepared for removal to be sent to the pathologist for examination.
Following the removal of the specimen, the surgeon begins the reconstruction process to restore gastrointestinal continuity, ensuring that ingested food will pass through the intestines properly.

The surgeon lifts the transverse colon to locate the small bowel, identifying the proximal part at the ligament of Treitz. The small bowel is followed down to a location that can reach the esophagus. A window is created in the mesentery, a veil of fatty tissue containing blood vessels, to facilitate this connection.

The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the small bowel, then lifts the transverse colon to create a window in the mesentery, preparing to feed one end of the divided bowel up to the esophagus. The mesentery appears as a translucent area beneath the transverse colon.

The surgeon passes the small bowel through the created hole in the mesentery of the transverse colon, then flips the transverse colon down to locate the small bowel. Additional small bowel is pulled through to ensure sufficient length to reach the esophagus. The next step involves making a connection between the esophagus and the elevated small bowel.

The surgeon creates a small hole in the small intestine and another in the esophagus. A surgical stapler is inserted, with one half positioned in the small intestine and the other half in the esophagus. Upon firing the stapler, a connection is formed between the esophagus and the small intestine.

The surgeon completes the connection between the esophagus and the small bowel, ensuring the esophagus remains in the abdomen. The lower esophagus is divided above the stomach level, and the entire stomach is removed. The remaining hole in the connection between the small bowel and esophagus is visible and needs closure.

The surgeon closes the connection between the esophagus and the small bowel using sutures. Starting from the right corner of the staple line, the suture is sewn towards the center. Another suture is initiated at the opposite corner of the staple line, tied, and sewn towards the center.

The surgeon completes the first layer of closure by tying sutures in the middle, followed by a second layer of sutures through the small bowel and esophagus to reinforce the connection and prevent leaks.

The surgeon creates a hole in the small bowel and connects it to another limb of the small bowel using a stapler. The stapler is run through each piece of intestine, and upon firing, it creates a single common opening to restore continuity.
The surgeon closes the connection between the esophagus and the small intestine with sutures, applying a two-layered closure technique. The final step involves placing a feeding tube.

The surgeon tacks the jejunum to the abdominal wall and passes a suture through the abdominal wall at multiple points to prevent bowel twisting. A hole is made in the small intestine, and a feeding tube is inserted through the abdominal wall into the jejunum. The tube is secured with sutures to prevent leakage, ensuring it is completely covered. The bowel is visibly moving, indicating readiness for function post-surgery.
